steps.text: Request information from the employer regarding the electrically classified locations at the facility.
Inspect the classified locations in the facility to ensure these locations are properly classified and that boundary distances have been adequately defined.
Ensure that electrical equipment located in classified areas is intrinsically safe, approved, or safe for the locations as required by 29 CFR 1910.307(c).
Revise policies and procedures for new material safety reviews.
Develop and implement policies and procedures for safety reviews of engineering projects.
Identify West manufacturing facilities that use combustible dusts.
Improve hazard communication programs so that the hazards of combustible dust are clearly identified and communicated to the workforce.
Develop procedures to maintain safety during non-routine operating conditions, such as the loss of oven temperature control.
Revise the incident investigation program to ensure that the underlying causes of incidents such as fires are identified and corrective actions implemented.

Develop a combustible dust safety program using good practice guidelines, such as NFPA 654, Standard for the Prevention of Fire and Dust Explosions from the Manufacturing, Processing, and Handling of Combustible Particulate Solids.
Minimize surfaces where combustible dust could accumulate in the design or modification of the plant.
Ensure phenolic resin-handling facilities are designed to prevent the spread of fires or explosions involving combustible dust.
Prevent the unsafe accumulation and dispersion of combustible dust by frequently cleaning process areas, including locations above production lines.
Minimize the dispersion of combustible dust by using appropriate dust-cleaning methods and tools.
Address the dangers of combustible dust and the prevention of dust explosions in the hazard communication training program.
Have you received the required training to perform a combustible dust inspection?
Have you reviewed the current NEP?
Do you have the PPE and sampling supplies needed for inspection?
Consider the hazards and characteristics of the combustible dust(s) expected at the site.
Determine and be familiar with the applicable OSHA standards.
Review Appendix C, Common Inspection Questions section of this chapter.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the incident reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the OSHA 300 forms and/or other reports.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedures and work practices.
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the procedure(s).
Ask managers and employees for a copy of the documents.
Inspect, test, and maintain combustible dust-handling and dust-related equipment and systems in accordance with industry standards.
Obtain a copy of the ignition control program from managers and employees.
Ensure electrical equipment and devices are intrinsically safe, approved for, or safe for the Class II locations.
Post 'No Smoking' signs in areas where smoking is prohibited.
Implement a hot work permit program if hot work is done.
Utilize grounding and bonding, and other methods for dissipating electrostatic charge.
Select and use powered industrial trucks that are approved for the combustible dust locations.
Ensure personnel who perform manual emptying or filling of containers or vessels are grounded during these operations.
Follow guidance for dusts with an MIE less than or equal to 30 mJ.
Avoid the formation of a dust cloud during the sampling activity.
Utilize bonding and grounding procedures.
Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing.
Transfer the contents of the scoop into a 1-liter sampling container.
Repeat until the 1-liter sampling container is nearly full. Close the lid tightly and wipe off excess dust that may be clinging to the bottle.
Apply the sample seal.
Remove the heel grounders and bonding wires.
Notify the employer that sampling is complete.
Don the heel grounders prior to sampling.
Connect the hand activated clamp end of the scoop bonding wire to a nearby grounded metal surface (e.g., structural steel beam or metal frame of equipment bolted directly to the floor).
Attach the thumb screw end of the scoop bonding wire to the handle of the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Check that all systems are visually bonded.
Utilize a natural bristle brush or your clean hand to gently sweep the dust into the aluminum, conductive hand scoop.
Call the AO and discuss the concerns with your AAD and AD.
Consult with your RO to determine how to proceed if it's too dangerous to sample.
Determine if the sample can be collected from another location or container.
Ensure approval by the AO and RO before collecting samples of low-MIE combustible dusts from containers or equipment not covered.

preconditions.text: If not trained, contact your supervisor for assistance from a CSHO having the required combustible dust training.
If not reviewed, STOP and review the current NEP.
If PPE and sampling supplies are not obtained, obtain the PPE and sampling supplies before proceeding.

constraints.text: PPE must include a hard hat, safety glasses, and FRC.
Supplies must include 1-liter sample bottles, an aluminum scoop, a natural bristle brush, a measuring tool, pen and paper, and a camera.
Additional PPE and sampling supplies are necessary when low-MIE combustible dusts are present.
Consult with your Region and HRT before sampling combustible dusts associated with 3D printing operations utilizing metal powders.
When sampling dusts, contact supervisor if other hazards are suspected.
Wear PPE including hard hat, safety glasses, and FR clothing.
Use of the HRT for site visits to perform sampling or other activities must be approved by the RO.
An imminent hazard notice may be appropriate if an imminent hazard exists.
Fire and/or explosion risk due to potential ignition sources from upstream operations.
